acceptance_targets: [ch10_craft_001] Pass if a cold read of snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:549-552 produces the same dating as snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:325-335, with no 2004 reference or second incompatible year. Fail if the notebook still dates the Cape trip a year earlier than Sarah does.

[ch10_dialogue_001] Pass if the revised passages at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:191-193 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:537 no longer use the distinctive phrase "good different" or a recognizable reconstruction of the example-line sequence, and the two passages sound like different speakers with different relationships to Dominic. Fail if the example-line wording or an obvious near-copy still remains.

[ch10_prose_001] Pass if no more than two of those four sites still use the "Not ... Just ..." emphasis pattern and each revised beat remains legible on a cold read for its local dramatic function. Fail if three or more of the cited moments still rely on the same formula.

[F02] At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4177 and 4181, the revised lines must: (1) not contain the phrase 'didn't know who that was' in any form; (2) preserve Ray's directness and the dramatic function (confronting Nate's professional incompetence); (3) sound like Ray's established register (short, practical, slightly profane); (4) the two instances must use different phrasing from each other — no verbatim self-repetition.

[F18] Chapter 10 must: (1) contain no more than 1 instance of the 'Do you?' challenge format; (2) contain no more than 1 use of 'landed' to describe dialogue impact; (3) each character's challenge to Nate must sound distinct from the others — Sarah should not challenge with the same words Ray uses.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if a cold read of snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:549-552 produces the same dating as snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:325-335, with no 2004 reference or second incompatible year. Fail if the notebook still dates the Cape trip a year earlier than Sarah does.
findings.problem: The chapter establishes the Cape trip as "last August" in an October 2005 timeline, then writes Nate's notebook entry as "Cape trip Aug 2004." Because the notebook is the chapter's culminating survival document, the wrong year reads as a continuity contradiction and undercuts the scene's central proof that Nate is finally recording what he has learned.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:549-552 so the notebook's Cape entry matches the timing already fixed at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:325-335. If Nate is uncertain, keep the uncertainty on the restaurant name, oysters, or other missing details, not on the year the scene has already pinned down.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised passages at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:191-193 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:537 no longer use the distinctive phrase "good different" or a recognizable reconstruction of the example-line sequence, and the two passages sound like different speakers with different relationships to Dominic. Fail if the example-line wording or an obvious near-copy still remains.
findings.problem: The chapter leaks calibration-only style-bible language into the text. Marco's "You seem different" followed by Nate's internal "Good different," plus the notebook note that Renata "calls them good," reconstructs the banned example-line logic closely enough to read as template reuse rather than lived dialogue and distinct character memory.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:191-193 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:537 so the beat remains that other people notice Nate-as-Dominic has changed, but Marco says it in Marco's own bar-friend register and Nate's note about Renata records her habit without echoing the calibration phrasing.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if no more than two of those four sites still use the "Not ... Just ..." emphasis pattern and each revised beat remains legible on a cold read for its local dramatic function. Fail if three or more of the cited moments still rely on the same formula.
findings.problem: The chapter leans repeatedly on the same emphasis scaffold: "Not X. Not Y. Just Z." / "Not X. Just Y." It works once, but by the third and fourth use the prose starts announcing emphasis through a reusable template instead of moment-specific rhythm.
findings.rewrite_direction: Recast at least two of the cited passages at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:155, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:313-314, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:355-356, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:507 so Marco's scrutiny, Sarah's stillness, Sarah's withdrawal, and Ray's exit land through different sentence architectures rather than the same negation ladder.

findings.acceptance_test: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4177 and 4181, the revised lines must: (1) not contain the phrase 'didn't know who that was' in any form; (2) preserve Ray's directness and the dramatic function (confronting Nate's professional incompetence); (3) sound like Ray's established register (short, practical, slightly profane); (4) the two instances must use different phrasing from each other — no verbatim self-repetition.
findings.problem: Style-bible example-line leakage. Ray's dialogue '"You didn't know who that was"' at lines 4177 and 4181 is a verbatim reproduction of the style-bible calibration line for ray_dunlap: 'CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: "You didn't know who that was." [not a question — a statement, after the client call]'. The line appears twice in the same exchange, doubling the exposure. The calibration label explicitly prohibits reproduction.
findings.rewrite_direction: Replace Ray's lines at snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4177 and 4181. Ray's challenge must still convey that Nate failed to recognize a key client, but in different words. Options: (a) Ray states the specific consequence ('That was Halpern. Your file. Your client. And you looked at him like a stranger.'); (b) Ray makes it personal ('You blanked on your own client, man.'); (c) Ray asks a question that is really an accusation ('How do you not know Halpern?'). At line 4181, the repeated version should be rewritten as a follow-up that escalates rather than restates — e.g., Ray naming what the failure means for Nate's position.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 10 must: (1) contain no more than 1 instance of the 'Do you?' challenge format; (2) contain no more than 1 use of 'landed' to describe dialogue impact; (3) each character's challenge to Nate must sound distinct from the others — Sarah should not challenge with the same words Ray uses.
findings.problem: Two related dialogue-voice issues converge in Chapter 10. (1) 'Do you?' template: the two-word challenge 'Do you?' appears at lines 4071 (Sarah) and 4191 (Ray) within 120 lines. This exact exchange format ('I know.' / 'Do you?') appears 9 times across the novel (also at lines 1004, 1288, 3402, 3470, 4936, 5960, 7652). Three different characters use the identical construction, which should be specific to a single voice if it is to register as character rather than formula. (2) 'Landed' tic: the word appears at lines 4215 ('The word landed and kept landing'), 4766 ('The word landed hard enough that he almost flinched'), and 4862 ('The last part landed harder because it was accurate') — three uses in one chapter, the densest cluster in the novel.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md: (a) Replace the 'Do you?' at line 4071 or 4191 with a challenge specific to that character's voice. Sarah's version could be a pointed silence, a specific counter-question ('Since when?'), or a facial reaction that Nate reads. Ray's version could be more direct ('Bullshit you do.'). Keep at most one 'Do you?' per chapter. (b) Reduce the 'landed' uses from 3 to 1. Keep the strongest (4766 or 4215); replace the others with different verbs for how language registers — 'hit,' 'caught,' or simply cut the narrator's impact commentary and let the dialogue register through Nate's physical or behavioral response.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel's central premise involves consciousness murder, identity theft, sexual fraud, and the demolition of a working-class community for profit. It must not flinch from any of these. The sex scenes between Nate and Sarah must reckon with the fact that Sarah is being deceived about who is in the bed with her — this is not a comfortable situation and the prose should not make it comfortable. The grief scenes must be physically specific — no 'he was devastated,' only the concrete gestures and sensory details of a man watching his life's work get torn down. The guilt should live in Nate's body (Dominic's body), not in tidy moral reflections. When the novel is funny, it should be funny about things that are actually terrible — the comedy should never feel safe. When Nate discovers that Dominic was trying to help, the reader should feel sick, not enlightened. Risk specificity over tastefulness at every turn.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism in place of specificity is a craft failure. When a scene requires difficult content — the moral weight of having killed an innocent man, the complicated ethics of sex under false identity, the physical reality of a man losing his livelihood, the specific humiliation of professional incompetence, the material texture of gambling debts and their enforcement — render it with the same care and specificity given to any other scene. The reader should feel the roughness of the situation, not a smoothed-over summary of it.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or Victor's professional register when delivering serious messages. If a line reads as stiff or literary, check whether contractions would make it sound spoken.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate chapter energy aggressively. A comic workplace chapter should not be followed by another comic workplace chapter. A grief-heavy scene should be followed by something that lets the reader breathe — not through tonal whiplash but through a genuine shift in the kind of pressure being applied. Vary scene counts, dialogue-to-narration ratios, and temporal compression. Some chapters should cover hours; others should cover weeks. At least three chapters should contain extended quiet: meals, walks, conversations about nothing important. These are not filler — they are the human baseline that makes the high-pressure scenes land.
prose_style_profile.diction: Plain American English with a slightly elevated attention to physical specificity. Not minimalist — the prose has room for interiority and texture — but never ornate. Latinate vocabulary only when the concept demands it. Prefer the concrete noun over the abstract one, the specific tool name over the generic 'tool,' the actual menu item over 'food.' The prose should feel like a smart person thinking clearly, not a writer crafting sentences.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, limited to Nate. The camera sits inside Nate's skull — we see through his eyes, think in his rhythms, and register the world through his specific sensory and emotional biases. The narration should reflect Nate's intelligence and his limitations simultaneously: he notices things (sharp observer) but misinterprets them (blinded by resentment, guilt, and self-pity). The narrative should not explain things Nate wouldn't articulate and should not achieve insights Nate hasn't earned.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied sentence length with a center of gravity around 15-20 words. Short declaratives for landing beats. Longer, accumulative sentences for sensory description and interior processing. Avoid sustained staccato (more than three short sentences in sequence) and sustained lyricism (more than two long, clause-heavy sentences in sequence). The prose should breathe.
